ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kung Pao Chicken; mapped to closest parent 'chicken dish' class (FOODON_03311069) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific Kung Pao Chicken entry; A0CKM (Chicken meat dish) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for Kung Pao Chicken as a prepared dish; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kung Pao Chicken in Singapore is approximately 200 g, served as a shared side dish alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Kung Pao Chicken — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kung Pao Chicken (宫保鸡丁), which is the canonical Singapore-region approved primary source for this dish. All per-100g values are taken from this record with confidence 0.95 as indicated in the input metadata.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed chicken stir-fry with vegetables and moderate starch thickener
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kung Pao Chicken in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the closest analogous food: mixed chicken and vegetable stir-fry with light starch-thickened sauce, which has a GI in the range of 40–50 due to the dominant protein and fat matrix slowing glucose absorption. Carbohydrate per serving (200 g × 6.87 g/100 g = 13.74 g available carbohydrate). GL = (45 × 13.74) / 100 = 6.2 per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 402 mg per 100 g from soy-based sauce
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 402 mg/100 g sits at the lower boundary of the borderline range (400–600 mg); individuals with hypertension should request reduced-soy-sauce preparation and limit portion size to keep total sodium intake within the 2,000 mg/day target, while retaining peanuts for their potassium (218 mg/100 g) offset benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (6.87 g/100 g), high protein (9.76 g/100 g), low estimated GI (~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content, high protein from chicken and peanuts, 1.5 g/100 g dietary fibre, and estimated GI of 45 collectively support glycaemic stability; however, cornstarch thickener adds refined carbohydrate and the dish should be consumed without white rice to avoid a significant increase in total meal glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 402 mg/100 g and cholesterol 26 mg/100 g; partially offset by unsaturated fats from peanuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The moderate sodium level and presence of cholesterol (26 mg/100 g) are relevant for cardiovascular risk management; peanuts contribute unsaturated fatty acids that are cardioprotective, but portion control and reduced-sodium preparation are recommended for individuals with established cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade ingredients (dried chilli, bell pepper) and sodium 402 mg/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried chilli and bell pepper are nightshade vegetables containing solanine, which may aggravate inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; request chilli-free preparation and note that peanuts provide anti-inflammatory vitamin E and healthy fats as a partial positive factor.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kung Pao Chicken (宫保鸡丁, Gōng Bǎo Jī Dīng) is a classic Chinese stir-fried dish originating from Sichuan province and widely adapted across China and Southeast Asia, including Singapore where it appears on Chinese restaurant and hawker menus as a popular lunch and dinner option. The dish consists of bite-sized pieces of marinated chicken (breast or thigh), dry-roasted peanuts, dried red chillies, and Sichuan peppercorns, stir-fried at high heat in a wok with a savoury-sweet-sour sauce made from soy sauce, rice vinegar, sugar, and cornstarch. The Beijing-style rendition (京派宫保鸡丁) featured in this record uses a slightly milder spice profile compared to the original Sichuan version, with less numbing peppercorn heat and a more balanced sauce. The texture is tender and juicy chicken with crunchy peanuts and a glossy, lightly thickened sauce coating. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 129 kcal, 9.76 g protein, 6.98 g fat, 6.87 g carbohydrate, 1.5 g dietary fibre, and 402 mg sodium. The protein content from chicken and peanuts supports satiety and muscle maintenance. The estimated glycaemic index is approximately 45 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when consumed without white rice. The sodium level at 402 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management and reduced-soy-sauce preparation is advisable. Peanuts contribute potassium (218 mg/100 g), magnesium (24 mg/100 g), and unsaturated fats with cardioprotective properties. The dish contains peanut and soy allergens and is not suitable for individuals with peanut allergy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Kung Pao Chicken?
Is Kung Pao Chicken suitable for diabetics?
Is Kung Pao Chicken high in sodium?
What is the difference between Kung Pao and Gong Bao chicken?
Does Kung Pao Chicken contain peanuts?
Can I eat Kung Pao Chicken if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 as recorded in the input metadata. HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore-region dishes. The food name, food group, and subgroup are consistent with the source. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence sub-component) but all other fields are directly supported.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this Kung Pao Chicken preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16; USDA FoodData Central was also consulted but does not provide a sufficiently close match for this specific Singapore-style preparation to substitute these values. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.2 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central reference for stir-fried chicken with peanuts and oil (closest approved-source analogue); this is an approximation and should be verified against a full lipid panel if available. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework applied to mixed chicken stir-fry with light starch sauce; no direct GI measurement for Kung Pao Chicken was found in the database. Input description contained typographical errors ('ond' for 'and', 'Includod' for 'Included') which have been corrected in the output. The input condition key 'diabetes' has been mapped to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. Ontology match types set to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn and FoodEx2 as no exact term exists for Kung Pao Chicken in either system.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name to canonical English 'Kung Pao Chicken' (removed Chinese prefix from primary name field).
Added mandatory multilingual local_names: chinese_simplified, chinese_traditional, malay, tamil, hindi per G6 Singaporean dish rules.
Expanded aliases to 13 entries covering all required language forms and English variants.
Populated aliases_by_language with all language keys and verified consistency with flat aliases array.
Added saturated_fat_g (1.2, estimated from USDA analogue) to satisfy Q3 secondary nutrient completeness.
Added GI (45, analogous estimate), gi_category, glycemic_load_per_serving, and gi_source to satisfy Q4.
Mapped input 'diabetes' condition to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas naming rules.
Added cardiovascular_disease health context entry (not present in input) to satisfy Q7.
Populated ontology with defensible closest-parent FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes with honest gap notes.
Populated full llm_training section with >200-character description, common queries, meal context, and ingredient synonyms.

Fixed g3: documented all -1 sentinel fields in notes with explicit reason (not reported in HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-16) and citation of USDA FoodData Central as secondary source also consulted, per G3 negative sentinel values rule.

Fixed g5: corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent' to comply with allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] per validator feedback.
Corrected typographical errors from input description.
Updated notes to explicitly list all -1 fields and state the reason data is unavailable and sources consulted, satisfying the negative sentinel values critical rule.
